note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence/clause boundaries (<=~95 chars). Block windows measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-10-06-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM): the Sigma(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 176.599s but the ffprobed master is 176.196s (0.40s off, past the +-0.3s gate). Sequential normalized cross-correlation (full-block probe, local search) locks every block at ncc=1.000 with a consistent ~0.36s inter-block gap; the master is the 11 blocks concatenated with that gap after each. Per-file ffprobe durations are inflated ~0.037s each by mp3 encoder-delay padding that the PCM concat strips, and the error accumulates to -0.40s by s11, which is the whole discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset (a multiple of the 0.08s mp3-frame grid: s01 0.00, s02 10.88, s03 26.40, s04 40.64, s05 60.88, s06 74.64, s07 90.72, s08 107.84, s09 125.20, s10 139.60, s11 157.12); each endS is the next block's onset; the final end = ffprobed master duration 176.196s.

phrases.text: Open the site's own status board.
One location, pulled onto one screen.
The trail up top shows you drilled in from the network center,
and the back link runs you straight back there when you're done.
Start with the header.
Site, status, address, the account it belongs to, and who manages it, all in a single strip.
The sync time tells you how current this is.
Right now it's a couple of minutes old, and the status reads critical.
Three buttons sit to the right.
These are your fast responses: open a job, ping the account manager,
or push the site up the priority list.
Each one kicks off something real, so today we read them and keep our hands off.
Now the numbers.
Six tiles across, and every one carries its own yardstick.
Washes and revenue today against their averages.
Wait time and service level against target.
And the count of open incidents, which is where the trouble surfaces.
You read today's figure next to its benchmark, without doing the math yourself.
Middle of the page, the equipment panel.
Eight units at this wash, one card each,
every card showing a few live readings and a health score.
It's the hardware view of the site, and any card drills deeper on a click.
Look at the first card.
The point-of-sale is offline.
No health score, no transactions in forty-seven minutes, a red bar flagging it.
When the P-O-S is down the site takes no money, so this is the card you check first.
Sweep the rest.
Conveyor, scanner, chemicals, dryers, water, gate, lighting.
Most run green and healthy; a couple sit lower and earn a second look.
The health number and the color bar do your triage at a glance.
On the right, the open incidents.
Three of them, each tagged by type and, just as usefully, by owner.
The payments outage is on the account manager.
The conveyor stop is still unassigned.
That owner line tells you what already has a name on it and what's waiting for one.
Underneath, the timeline.
Every event with a timestamp, from the shift opening this morning
to the terminal dropping offline later on.
This is the site's story of the day.
When you need to know what led up to an incident, you scroll here.
Bottom right, the quick actions.
The common responses, gathered so you don't go hunting:
raise a work order, book maintenance, send a tech, email the customer, restart the P-O-S.
Every one does something real, so we read the list and stop there.
Step back and take it in.
One page: status up top, then the numbers, the hardware,
the open incidents with owners, the day's history, and the actions that answer them.
Read it top to bottom and you know this site cold,
and you did it without touching a thing.
